title: Spinning from a batt on the wheel
subtitle: Strip or side-feed a carded batt for a lofty woollen singles
excerpt: A carded batt can go straight onto the spinning wheel without being wound into roving first. Strip it into pencil-thick lengths by hand, or feed directly from the side of the batt using a woollen long-draw. Both approaches produce a lofty, airy singles with the character of the blended fibre intact.
sourceNotes: Observation-led. Batt-spinning methods follow the conventional guild teaching for woollen-preparation spinning: strip-to-pencil-roving and side-feed are both documented in Alden Amos, The Alden Amos Big Book of Handspinning (referenced for substance). The WPI calibration process follows standard guild practice for measuring finished singles and plied yarns.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of carded fibre, typically produced on a drum carder or commercial carding machine. The fibres are loosely aligned in multiple directions rather than parallel, giving the finished yarn a lofty, airy character distinct from a worsted-drawn singles spun from combed top.

A drafting technique in which the spinner draws the fibre back at an angle away from the orifice, allowing twist to run into the drafting zone while the fibres are still separating. Suited to short-staple and lofty preparations such as rolags and batts. The resulting yarn has more air trapped between fibres and greater elasticity than a worsted-spun yarn.

Wraps per inch: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler or gauge over one inch (2.5 cm) without stretching or overlapping. Used to classify yarn weight: lace is 30+ WPI, fingering around 20, DK around 12, aran around 9, bulky 7 and below.

A single strand of yarn spun from prepared fibre before any plying. A singles has active twist and will kink back on itself if allowed to relax unsupported. Most spinning projects ply two or more singles together to balance the twist and produce a stable yarn.

attrs.title: Wheel safety and wool dust
attrs.body: Keep hair tied back and sleeves clear of the drive band and flyer. Pulling batts apart releases fine dust: work in a ventilated room.

content.text: A
batt

comes off the drum carder as a layered sheet: fibres crossing at low angles, air pockets between the layers, and a lofty hand that feels nothing like a combed top. That structure is the point. Spin it worsted and you lose half the character; the short-staple fibres worked against the parallel alignment pull unevenly and the resulting yarn is neither smooth nor airy. The right pairing is a
woollen draw
, where twist runs into the drafting zone while the fibres are still separating, trapping air and producing an elastic
singles
suited to knitted fabrics that need bounce.

Two methods work from a batt: stripping it by hand into pencil-thick lengths before sitting at the wheel, or feeding directly from the side of the batt while spinning (the side-feed method). Both produce a woollen-character yarn, but they suit different fibre blends and different spinners. Strip-and-spin gives more control when the batt is dense or contains long fibres that resist drafting mid-session. Side-feed is faster and works well when the batt is light and well-blended.
What you need

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony), set to a mid-range whorl for 6:1 to 8:1 twist ratio
Niddy-noddy for winding off the hank
Soft tape measure or WPI gauge for checking yarn weight
Hand carders (optional, to re-card any dense patches in the batt)

content.text: Preparing the batt

Before spinning, decide which preparation method suits the batt in your hands. Pull one corner of the batt gently. If the fibres draft smoothly with light tension, the batt is open enough for side-feed. If they resist or tear in clumps, strip first.
Step 1: Strip the batt (strip-and-spin method)

Lay the batt flat. Tear a strip from one short end, pulling perpendicular to the fibre layers, about 2-3 cm wide. Gently pre-draft this strip between both hands by holding it 20 cm apart and drawing your hands slowly apart until the strip narrows to roughly pencil thickness. Do not draft so far that the strip breaks; leave it slightly denser than the final spinning draft. Fold the pre-drafted length loosely and set it aside. Tear the next strip. Work through the whole batt before sitting at the wheel so the session is uninterrupted.
Step 2: Prepare the wheel

Set the wheel to a whorl that gives 6:1 to 8:1 twist ratio. A lofty batt singles needs less twist than a combed top: too much twist compresses the fibres and defeats the airy structure. Set brake tension light, so the take-up is gentle. A batt singles is fragile until the twist is distributed evenly; a strong take-up snaps the draft before it stabilises.
Spinning the singles
Step 3: Spin with a long woollen draw

Join the pre-drafted strip onto the leader with a short overlap. Hold the strip in the back hand, palm facing up. The front hand acts as a pinch gate just in front of the orifice, holding the twist back. Treadle steadily to build Z-twist in the leader, then open the front hand and draw the back hand away from the orifice in a long, controlled arc: 30-40 cm in one movement. Twist will run up into the drawing zone as you extend the draft. Once the draw is complete, close the front hand again to stop the twist and let the take-up advance the yarn onto the bobbin. This is the long woollen draw: one open, arcing movement rather than a series of short pulls.

For side-feed, hold the batt loosely in the back hand and tear a small amount of fibre from its side edge as you draw back. The batt hangs from the front of your back hand; gravity keeps it from tangling in the wheel. The drafting movement is the same long arc, but you are feeding directly from the batt mass rather than from a pre-drafted strip. Control how much fibre enters the draft by adjusting how firmly you grip the batt edge.
Step 4: Monitor the yarn as you spin
Aim for a singles at 18-22
WPI

for a 2-ply that will land at DK to aran weight (10-14 WPI plied). To check: stop treadling, pinch the yarn near the orifice, and let a short length hang free. A well-spun batt singles from a medium-staple blend holds its shape without kinking back aggressively. If it coils tightly the moment you release tension, the twist count is too high: reduce the treadle rate or increase your drawing speed.

Inconsistency in diameter is normal when spinning from a batt, especially early in a session. Batts are not uniform: some zones have more fibre, some less. Work with the variation rather than against it by adjusting the back-hand draw speed to match what the fibre is giving. Thick sections draw back quickly; thin sections need a slower draw to avoid breaking.
Step 5: Fill a second bobbin

Spin a second bobbin at the same settings. When spinning batts in a blend of multiple fibres, work through the batts in the same order for both bobbins so that the colour or fibre transitions in the finished ply stay in step. If colour sequence doesn't matter, simply fill both bobbins until the batt is used.
Plying and setting the twist

Place both bobbins on a lazy kate. Ply with S-twist (treadle the opposite direction from spinning) at a lower twist ratio, around 4:1 to 5:1. Batt singles are lofty and need less ply twist than a dense combed-top yarn: over-plying collapses the air structure that makes the batt worth spinning. To check balance, let a 30 cm length hang free from your hand after a few treadles of ply. A balanced 2-ply hangs straight. If it twists clockwise, add more ply twist; if it twists anti-clockwise, reduce it.

Wind the plied yarn onto the niddy-noddy. Tie the hank in three places with waste yarn. Soak in lukewarm water for 20 minutes, press gently in a towel, and snap the hank once between your hands. Hang to dry with no tension or a very light weight. Lofty woollen yarns bloom slightly on drying: the finished WPI will be slightly lower (thicker) than the wet measurement, so check WPI only after the hank is fully dry.

Measure WPI after drying: wrap the yarn around a ruler for one inch without stretching. A DK-weight 2-ply batt yarn sits at 10-12 WPI; aran sits at 8-10 WPI. If the yarn is significantly finer than planned, the singles were drawn too thin: adjust the batt strip width and draw speed on the next batch.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The singles break repeatedly during the long draw
items.cause: The batt strip is too thin or too loosely pre-drafted before spinning, so the drafting zone runs out of fibre before the twist stabilises the strand.
items.fix: Increase the strip width by 1-2 cm and pre-draft less aggressively. The strip should feel slightly thicker than you think is right before it joins the orifice. If breaks continue at the same point, check that the brake tension is not pulling the yarn onto the bobbin faster than the twist is building.

items.problem: The plied yarn is limp and loses loft after washing
items.cause: Over-plying collapsed the air trapped in the woollen singles. Or the twist-setting water was too hot and the fibre fulled slightly.
items.fix: Reduce ply twist ratio to 4:1 or lower. For twist-setting, use lukewarm water, not hot. A gentle 15-minute soak rather than a vigorous snap is enough for a lofty batt singles.

items.problem: The finished yarn is uneven in thickness throughout the skein
items.cause: Batts are not uniform in density. Inconsistent drawing speed makes the variation worse.
items.fix: Some unevenness is normal for batt-spun yarn. For gross unevenness, re-card the batt on hand carders before stripping to even the fibre density. Minor variation is not a flaw.

items.problem: The singles kinks back into a tight coil when taken off the bobbin
items.cause: Too much Z-twist in the singles: the treadle rate was too fast for the drafting speed, so twist built up faster than fibre was fed.
items.fix: Increase drawing speed or reduce treadle rate. For batt spinning, 6:1 is usually enough. Spin a test length of 30 cm, let it hang free, and check that it twists back gently rather than snapping into a corkscrew.
